card.title: The 1% Better Rule
card.teaser: Improve by 1% a day for a year and you end up 37 times better. The math is almost unfair.
card.coverImagePrompt: A staircase of tiny identical steps climbing up out of morning fog into bright blue sky, painterly, cinematic, motivational yet restrained

lessons.title: The quiet arithmetic of tiny gains
lessons.body: Here's a line that looks like motivational poster junk until you do the math: if you get 1% better every day for a year, you end up roughly 37 times better. 1.01 raised to the 365th power is 37.78. That's not a metaphor. That's just compounding — the same force that makes small savings turn into retirements.

visual.imagePrompt: A gently curving exponential line rising from lower left to upper right across a grid, minimal, elegant

lessons.title: What James Clear called 'atomic habits'
lessons.body: In his 2018 book, writer James Clear borrowed a metaphor from physics. An atom is tiny, but also a source of enormous energy. An 'atomic' habit is small enough to do on a bad day, but repeated often enough to reshape who you become. The point is identity, not effort.

keyTerms.definition: A very small, routine behavior that compounds into significant change over time
Growth where each gain itself generates further gain, producing exponential curves

visual.imagePrompt: A glowing atom symbol orbited by small daily-life icons: a book, sneakers, a water glass, a notebook

lessons.title: Why small beats ambitious
lessons.body: Big resolutions fail because they rely on motivation, which is finite. Tiny habits survive because they slip under the radar of resistance. Two pushups. One page. One honest sentence in a journal. The goal isn't the two pushups — it's proving to yourself that you are the kind of person who shows up, so that tomorrow doesn't start from zero.

visual.imagePrompt: A warm flat illustration of a single checkmark on a calendar, repeated across a grid of days

lessons.title: The British cycling experiment
lessons.body: Clear's favorite case study: in 2003, coach Dave Brailsford took over British Cycling, a team that had won just one Olympic gold in 76 years. He obsessed over 1% improvements — better massage gels, warmer overshorts, the exact pillow each rider slept on. By 2012, British riders had won 70% of available gold medals at the London Olympics. No single change did it. All of them, together, did.
lessons.funFact: Brailsford even had the team's truck painted white inside, so any speck of dust affecting bike performance would be immediately visible.

visual.imagePrompt: A velodrome cyclist mid-sprint, motion blur on the wheels, spotlighted against a dark crowd

lessons.title: Cast a vote for who you're becoming
lessons.body: Forget the annual goal for a second. Every small habit is a vote for an identity: 'I'm the kind of person who reads,' 'who trains,' 'who writes.' You don't need a majority of votes right away. You just need to win today's election. Do that often enough and the curve does the rest.

quiz.question: If you improve 1% each day, how many times better are you after a year?
quiz.explanation: 1.01 to the 365th power equals roughly 37.78 — the arithmetic of compounding.

quiz.question: Who wrote 'Atomic Habits'?
quiz.explanation: James Clear published 'Atomic Habits' in 2018, popularizing the 1% better framing.

quiz.question: According to Clear, the most durable driver of habit change is:
quiz.explanation: Clear argues that habits stick when they reinforce who you believe you are — not through raw motivation.

sources.title: Atomic Habits: An Easy & Proven Way to Build Good Habits & Break Bad Ones
How 1% Performance Improvements Led to Olympic Gold
